content:
First, label the elements of M in order as
  a₁,a₂,a₃,…  where we have the “blocks”  
    (a₂m₋₁,a₂m) = (3m−2, 3m−1),   m=1,2,3,….

We are summing 2n consecutive terms, say a_k+…+a_{k+2n−1}, and we want
  S= a_k+…+a_{k+2n−1} =300.

There are two cases, depending on whether k is odd or even.

1)  k=2r−1 (odd).  Then the 2n terms are exactly the n full blocks  
     m=r,r+1,…,r+n−1  
   so
     S = ∑_{m=r}^{r+n−1}[(3m−2)+(3m−1)]
       = ∑_{m=r}^{r+n−1}(6m−3)
       = 6·(r+…+r+n−1) −3n
       = 6·[n(2r+n−1)/2] −3n
       = 3n(2r+n−1) −3n
       = 3n(2r+n−2).
   Hence 3n(2r+n−2)=300 ⇒ n(2r+n−2)=100.

2)  k=2r (even).  Then the 2n terms are  
     a_{2r}=3r−1,  full blocks  m=r+1,…,r+n−1,  and  a_{2(r+n)−1}=3(r+n)−2.  
   A similar sum gives
     S = 3n(2r+n−1),
   so  3n(2r+n−1)=300 ⇒ n(2r+n−1)=100.

In either case we get an equation of the form
   n·A=100,  A an integer,  with  r=…=(A−n+2)/2 ≥1  or  r=(A−n+1)/2 ≥1.
Hence n must be a divisor of 100:

Divisors of 100 are 1,2,4,5,10,20,25,50,100.  Checking each in the two formulas one finds that the only n ≥ 1 which admit an integer r≥1 are

   n = 1, 2, 4, 5, 10.

Thus the possible values of n are

\boxed{1,2,4,5,10}.
